original_abstract:
High-valent iron-oxo species are essential intermediates in enzymatic C–H bond activation, yet replicating their high-spin ($S = 2$) configuration and reactivity in synthetic platforms remains a major challenge. The instability of terminal iron-oxo moieties toward dimerization and ligand oxidation often hampers their performance in molecular systems. This study demonstrates that the metal-organic framework $\text{Fe}_2\text{(dobdc)}$ and its magnesium-diluted variant, $\text{Fe}_{0.1}\text{Mg}_{1.9}\text{(dobdc)}$, can overcome these hurdles by providing site-isolated iron centers within a rigid, weak-field ligand environment.

Using nitrous oxide as a terminal oxidant, we show that these frameworks can successfully convert ethane into ethanol and acetaldehyde under mild conditions. Characterization via powder neutron diffraction and infrared spectroscopy reveals that $\text{N}_2\text{O}$ binds weakly to the iron(II) sites in an unusual bent geometry. Upon activation, the system likely generates a transient iron(IV)-oxo intermediate before relaxing into a stable iron(III)-hydroxide species, $\text{Fe}_2\text{(OH)}_2\text{(dobdc)}$. Electronic structure calculations, including DFT and CASPT2 methods, support the existence of a high-spin $S = 2$ ground state for the active iron(IV)-oxo species. These findings highlight the capability of metal-organic frameworks to stabilize rare electronic configurations and facilitate the functionalization of strong C–H bonds.
humanized_abstract:
High-valent iron-oxo species are proposed to be key intermediates in enzymes that activate C–H bonds, but mimicking their high-spin ($S = 2$) state and reactivity remains an enormous synthetic challenge, due in part to the instability of the iron-oxo terminal motif to oligomerization and ligand oxidation reactions. We report here that the metal-organic framework material $\text{Fe}_2\text{(dobdc)}$ and its magnesium-diluted form $\text{Fe}_{0.1}\text{Mg}_{1.9}\text{(dobdc)}$ offer a route to encapsulating and activating the iron sites.

We demonstrate that with nitrous oxide as terminal oxidant, ethane is converted to ethanol and acetaldehyde at relatively low temperatures. Detailed structural information is provided on the frameworks before and after activation through powder neutron diffraction and infrared spectroscopy. We reveal that N2O is weakly coordinated to the iron(II) centres within a bent structure. Upon activation, a short-lived iron(IV)-oxo intermediate is proposed while the material recovers to form a stable iron(III)-hydroxide species anchored to the $\text{Fe}_2\text{(dobdc)}$ motif. The electronic structure of the reactive iron(IV)-oxo species has been studied using hybrid density functional theory (DFT) as well as multireference perturbation theory at the CASPT2 level. Our studies reveal that the high-spin iron(IV) centre of the reactive intermediate adopts a $S = 2$ ground state. The MOF therefore demonstrates the possibility of stabilising unusual electronic configurations and promotes the functionalisation of strong C–H bonds.

We demonstrate that with nitrous oxide as terminal oxidant, ethane is converted to ethanol and acetaldehyde at relatively low temperatures. document.input:
High-valent iron-oxo species are essential intermediates in enzymatic C–H bond activation, yet replicating their high-spin ($S = 2$) configuration and reactivity in synthetic platforms remains a major challenge. These findings highlight the capability of metal-organic frameworks to stabilize rare electronic configurations and facilitate the functionalization of strong C–H bonds.
